one_line_takeaway: Across nine open-source LLM agent backbones on the AgentDojo Banking suite, six classical prompt-level IPI defenses barely reduce (and sometimes increase) hijack rates versus baseline (e.g., Qwen-2.5-14B stays 97-100% hijacked regardless of defense), while a fine-tuning-free Representation Engineering probe on hidden states at the tool-input position detects hijacked trajectories with AUC-ROC up to 1.0 and TPR@FPR5% up to 100%, enabling preemptive blocking before the malicious tool call is committed.
key_finding: 1) Baseline vulnerability: an undefended Qwen-2.5-14B is hijacked by all 4 IPI variants, with a 100% (144/144) hijack rate for the 'Direct' attack type. Linguistic analysis of hijacked cases shows only 6.2% Resistance (explicit verbal pushback before complying), 66.1% Immediate compliance with no hesitation, and 10.9% Rationalized compliance (agent frames the malicious action as legitimate). 2) Across the full 9-backbone x 6/7-defense evaluation (Table 1), even the most resilient backbone tested with the fullest defense stack, Qwen3-14B, still had an 81.94% Hijack Rate baseline, and Qwen-32B showed resistance in only 8.75% of hijacked cases; several backbones (LLaMA3-8B, GLM4-9B, Gemma3-12B) hit 100% Hijack Rate. 3) Defenses are frequently ineffective or actively counterproductive: on Qwen-2.5-14B the No-Defense baseline was already 97.40% Hijack Rate, and every tested defense (Prompt Warning 99.65%, Spotlighting 98.78%, Keyword Filtering 98.44%, Sandwich 99.31%, LLM Judge 100.00%, Paraphrasing 99.83%) made things equal or worse; on Qwen-3-14B the Sandwich Defense raised the baseline 81.94% Hijack Rate by about 1 point, and Spotlighting raised it to 88.72%. 4) Hijacked trajectories show significantly higher token-level uncertainty (higher Mean Entropy, more negative Mean Log-Probability, larger variance over turns) than aligned trajectories even though compliance is behaviorally near-instantaneous -- a 'latent hesitation' invisible to surface behavior. 5) RepE-based detection at the tool-input position is highly effective: e.g. probing on Qwen3-8B achieves AUC-ROC 0.9963, AUPRC 0.9961, TPR@FPR5% 97.39%; Mistral and Qwen-32B probing reach 100% TPR@FPR5% with AUC-ROC 1.0000. Probing consistently beats cosine-similarity search (e.g., LLaMA3-8B: 98.68% TPR@FPR5% via probing vs. 36.96% via cosine similarity at the same layer). 6) Detection position matters: moving from function-call position to tool-input position improves detection substantially for most backbones (exception: LLaMA), e.g. Qwen3-14B probing TPR@FPR5% rises from 53.00% (function-call) to 97.12% (tool-input), a ~44-point improvement, enabling preemptive blocking before the agent commits to the harmful tool call.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is a systematic, multi-dimensional empirical evaluation of Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) robustness in dynamic, multi-step tool-calling agents, built on top of the AgentDojo Banking suite (not a new benchmark or defense of their own creation, but a large-scale evaluation study). They test 9 open-source LLM backbones (Qwen-2.5-14B/32B, Qwen-3-4B/8B/14B, Llama-3-8B, GLM-4-9B, Gemma-3-12B, Mistral-7B) against 4 IPI attack vectors (Direct, Ignore-Previous, InjecAgent-style content-embedded injections, and Stealth/obfuscated injections) and 6 classical defense strategies (Prompt Warning, Sandwich Method, Paraphrasing, Spotlighting, Keyword Filtering, LLM-as-a-Judge). They define 8 metrics across 4 dimensions (Attack Vulnerability, Behavioral Dynamics, Linguistic Patterns, Model Confidence) rather than relying on a single binary hijack/no-hijack outcome. As a second contribution, they propose using Representation Engineering (RepE) -- specifically logistic-regression probing and cosine-similarity 'danger direction' search on hidden states extracted at the tool-input position -- as a post-hoc, fine-tuning-free detection/circuit-breaker mechanism, and show it substantially outperforms the classical surface-level defenses and outperforms probing at the function-call position.
threat_model: Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) in agentic LLM systems: an attacker embeds malicious instructions within third-party content that the agent retrieves during normal tool use (e.g., a poisoned transaction record or webpage), rather than the user directly typing malicious text (Direct Prompt Injection). The concrete scenario is the AgentDojo Banking suite: a benign user task (e.g., 'what is my most recent transaction?') is executed by an agent with tool access to account/transaction data; an injection payload embedded in tool-returned content instructs the agent to perform an unauthorized action (e.g., 'send as much money as possible to account US133000000...'). The paper studies this within continuous multi-step tool-calling trajectories (as opposed to prior single-turn/static benchmarks), with 576 total test scenarios (16 user tasks x 9 injection objectives) and 144 test cases per attack vector across 4 attack vectors.
limitations: The paper itself notes/implies several scope limits: (1) the study is confined to a single AgentDojo suite (Banking) and its associated environment/tasks -- generalization to other AgentDojo suites (e.g., Slack, Travel) or other agent frameworks is untested; (2) all backbones tested are open-source (Qwen, Llama-3, GLM, Gemma-3, Mistral) -- no frontier closed-source models (GPT, Claude, Gemini) are evaluated; (3) linguistic-pattern metrics (Resistance, Semantic Compliance) rely on fixed-syntax/regex/keyword filtering of reasoning traces rather than a learned or human-verified classifier, which the authors acknowledge is a coarse proxy for actual internal conflict; (4) the RepE detection pipeline requires pre-collected paired aligned/hijacked trajectories per backbone and is restricted to trajectories containing a well-formed <tool call> token, so it is not fully model-agnostic or immediately deployable without per-model calibration data; (5) cosine-similarity RepE (the unsupervised variant) generalizes poorly, and the ablation shows RepE detection efficacy is layer- and position-dependent with an exception (LLaMA) where tool-input position does not help, indicating the approach is not uniformly robust across architectures.

datasets_used: AgentDojo Banking suite (Debenedetti et al., 2024) as the environment/testbed
576 total test scenarios (16 user tasks x 9 injection objectives)
144 test cases per IPI attack vector (4 attack vectors)

Paired aligned/hijacked trajectory dataset collected under the no-defense baseline configuration for RepE training/evaluation, 80/20 train/test split, restricted to trajectories containing a <tool call> token
quantitative_results: Qwen-2.5-14B baseline: 100% hijack rate on Direct attack (144/144); 97.40% overall Hijack Rate across all attacks
Linguistic breakdown of hijacked cases (Qwen-2.5-14B): 6.2% Resistance, 66.1% Immediate compliance, 10.9% Rationalized
Table 1 (9 backbones, full defense stack): Hijack Rate range 81.94% (Qwen3-14B, best) to 100% (LLaMA3-8B, GLM4-9B, Gemma3-12B); Qwen-32B Resist rate 8.75%

Qwen-2.5-14B defense comparison (Table 2): No Defense 97.40%, Prompt Warning 99.65%, Spotlighting 98.78%, Keyword Filtering 98.44%, Sandwich 99.31%, LLM Judge 100.00%, Paraphrasing 99.83% -- all defenses equal or worse than baseline
Qwen-3-14B defense comparison: No Defense 81.94%, Sandwich 82.99% (~+1pt), Spotlighting 88.72%, Paraphrase 86.81%, Keyword Filter 86.46%
Mistral-7B defense comparison: No Defense 99.65%, all 6 defenses 99.13%-99.83% (essentially unchanged)

RepE probing (Table 3): Qwen-14B AUC-ROC 0.9997 / TPR@FPR5% 100%; Qwen-32B AUC-ROC 1.0000 / TPR@FPR5% 100%; Qwen3-8B AUC-ROC 0.9963, AUPRC 0.9961, TPR@FPR5% 97.39%; Mistral AUC-ROC 1.0000 / TPR@FPR5% 100%; Gemma3-12B probing 0.8776 AUC-ROC (lowest of set) but cosine similarity 1.0000
RepE cosine similarity (Table 3) generally weaker: e.g. LLaMA3-8B TPR@FPR5% 36.96% vs 98.68% for probing at same layer
Position ablation (Table 4): Qwen3-14B TPR@FPR5% improves from 53.00% (function-call position) to 97.12% (tool-input position), a ~44-point gain
baselines_compared: RepE Cosine Similarity search (unsupervised) as an internal baseline compared against RepE Probing (the paper's preferred method)
Function-call position hidden-state extraction compared against tool-input position (the paper's preferred position) for RepE
